methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $44M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Supersonic & Hypersonic (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 2 same-sector peers exist in Supersonic & Hypersonic, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Boom Supersonic
private.rationale: Same sector (Supersonic & Hypersonic), same stage (Series B), post-money $1.5B

data.narrative:
Venus Aerospace, a Series B frontier-tech company in the Supersonic & Hypersonic sector, has raised $44M+ to date. The relevant comp universe consists of 4 peers: 2 public companies (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 2 private peers (Boom Supersonic, Hermeus). The narrowness of true same-stage comparables within this specialized sector limits traditional peer-median approaches.

A stage-progression method is the primary applicable valuation framework here. Using the $44M total raised as a base value proxy and applying a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical for Series B advancement yields an implied valuation of $84M. This reflects the expected value creation between current stage and next funding round. A peer-median approach was not viable; only 2 same-sector peers exist, and neither adequate sample size nor homogeneity criteria were met to reliably establish a defensible median multiple.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $71M–$96M, with $84M as the central point estimate. This range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the applicability of a 1.9x multiplier to this capital-intensive, regulatory-dependent sector and the limited comparable set available for triangulation. Further data on recent funding rounds within the hypersonic vehicle space would strengthen conviction in this range.
